tlg0007.tlg056.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_in_work: Classical Athens and Late Roman Republic
tlg0007.tlg056.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Compares the greatest Greek orator, Demosthenes, with the greatest Roman orator, Cicero.

tlg0007.tlg079.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A fictionalized symposium featuring the Seven Sages of Greece at the court of Periander in Corinth.

tlg0007.tlg092.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A dialogue concerning the decline of oracular power across the Mediterranean during the Roman period.

tlg0007.tlg134.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A commentary on Plato's Timaeus, written for the instruction of Plutarch's contemporaries in the Roman world.

tlg0007.tlg136.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A critique of the internal inconsistencies of Stoic philosophy as practiced and taught in the Roman Empire.

tlg0007.tlg076.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A consolatory letter addressed to Apollonius following the death of his son, reflecting Roman-era social customs.

tlg0007.tlg095.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A dialogue (De cohibenda ira) between contemporaries on the management of emotions.

tlg0007.tlg025.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A biography of Cato the Elder, documenting his life and censorship in Rome.
tlg0007.tlg025.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Cato, who was born in -234, nearly three centuries before Plutarch's time.

tlg0007.tlg058.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of Mark Antony, focusing on the Roman civil wars and the end of the Republic.
tlg0007.tlg058.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Antony from his birth in -83 through his death in -30, over a century before Plutarch's time.

tlg0007.tlg060.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of Dion of Syracuse and his political struggles against the tyrants Dionysius I and II.
tlg0007.tlg060.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Dion (born c. -408), focusing on the mid-4th century BCE.

tlg0007.tlg051.perseus-eng1.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the attempted social and economic reforms of King Agis IV in Sparta.
tlg0007.tlg051.perseus-eng1.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the reign of Agis IV, which began in -245, roughly 300 years before Plutarch.

tlg0007.tlg080.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An essay on religious psychology and philosophy, reflecting the intellectual climate of the Roman Empire.
tlg0007.tlg080.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: This is a philosophical essay reflecting Plutarch's own views and the contemporary religious discourse of his era.

tlg0007.tlg089.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Antiquarian treatment of Egyptian religious tradition and the myth of Isis and Osiris.

tlg0007.tlg042.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents the life of Quintus Sertorius and his rebellion in Hispania against the Sullan regime.
tlg0007.tlg042.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Sertorius from his birth (c. -123) through his command in Spain.

tlg0007.tlg045.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents the life of Pompey the Great, his military campaigns, and the First Triumvirate.
tlg0007.tlg045.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs Pompey's life from his birth in -106 through his death in -48.

tlg0007.tlg011.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents the life of Marcus Furius Camillus, the 'Second Founder of Rome', during the Gallic invasions.
tlg0007.tlg011.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs events from the 5th and 4th centuries BCE, specifically Camillus's birth c. -446.

tlg0007.tlg020.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A comparison between the Corinthian general Timoleon and the Roman Lucius Aemilius Paullus.
tlg0007.tlg020.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The comparison spans the 4th and 2nd centuries BCE; Timoleon was born c. -411.

tlg0007.tlg027.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents the life of Philopoemen, the 'Last of the Greeks', and his leadership of the Achaean League.
tlg0007.tlg027.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Philopoemen from his birth c. -253.

tlg0007.tlg018.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents Timoleon's expedition to Sicily to overthrow tyrants and repel Carthaginians.
tlg0007.tlg018.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Timoleon from his birth in Corinth c. -411.

tlg0007.tlg044.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents the life of Agesilaus II, king of Sparta, and his campaigns in Asia Minor and Greece.
tlg0007.tlg044.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Agesilaus from his birth c. -444.

tlg0007.tlg043.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A comparison between the Roman rebel Sertorius and the Greek successor Eumenes of Cardia.
tlg0007.tlg043.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The comparison spans the 4th and 1st centuries BCE; Eumenes was born c. -362.

tlg0007.tlg021.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents the life of the Theban general Pelopidas and his role in the liberation of Thebes.
tlg0007.tlg021.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Pelopidas, born c. -410, focusing on the Theban hegemony.

tlg0551.tlg014.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Appian documents the series of wars between the Roman Republic and Mithridates VI of Pontus.
tlg0551.tlg014.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The narrative focuses on the First Mithridatic War beginning in -89, over two centuries before Appian's time.

tlg0551.tlg003.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This book covers the early Roman expansion and conflicts with various Italian tribes.
tlg0551.tlg003.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work deals with the early Republic's Italian conquests starting from the expulsion of the kings in -509.

tlg0551.tlg004.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Appian narrates the Samnite Wars and Rome's struggle for dominance in central Italy.
tlg0551.tlg004.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The narrative begins with the First Samnite War in -343, long before Appian's 2nd-century CE floruit.

tlg0551.tlg005.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents Roman interactions and conflicts with Gallic tribes, including the sack of Rome and Caesar's conquests.
tlg0551.tlg005.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The narrative includes the Gallic sack of Rome in -390 and subsequent wars through the 1st century BCE.

tlg0551.tlg002.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This book covers the legendary and historical period of the seven kings of Rome.
tlg0551.tlg002.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work begins with the traditional founding of Rome by Romulus in -753.

tlg0551.tlg011.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Appian documents the Macedonian Wars between Rome and the Antigonid dynasty.
tlg0551.tlg011.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The narrative begins with the First Macedonian War in -214, roughly 350 years before the author.

tlg0551.tlg017.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Appian details the internal Roman conflicts from the Gracchi to the fall of the Republic.
tlg0551.tlg017.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The narrative begins with the tribunate of Tiberius Gracchus in -133.

tlg0551.tlg010.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Covers Roman relations with Numidia, specifically the Jugurthine War.
tlg0551.tlg010.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The primary focus is the Jugurthine War which began in -112.

tlg0032.tlg015.perseus-eng1.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work (attributed to 'Old Oligarch' but traditionally Xenophon) analyzes the political system of the Athenian democracy.
tlg0032.tlg015.perseus-eng1.mentioned_time_reasoning: The text describes the Athenian naval empire and democracy during the Peloponnesian War era (-431).

tlg0032.tlg003.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The Oeconomicus is a Socratic dialogue set in Athens, focusing on the management of an Athenian household and estate.
tlg0032.tlg003.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The dialogue features Socrates (d. -399) and reflects the social and economic conditions of Athens during Xenophon's own lifetime.

tlg0032.tlg012.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A technical manual on the duties of a hipparch (cavalry commander) in the Athenian military system.
tlg0032.tlg012.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Written by Xenophon to provide practical advice for contemporary Athenian military officers during his own era.

tlg0099.tlg001.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Books 6-14 cover Italy, Sicily, Northern/Eastern Europe, and Greece under the early Roman Principate.
tlg0099.tlg001.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: Strabo describes the geography and political state of the world as it existed during his life under Augustus and Tiberius.

tlg0526.tlg001.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A comprehensive history of the Jewish people from creation through the outbreak of the war with Rome.
tlg0526.tlg001.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Josephus reconstructs the biblical narrative starting from the patriarchal era, long before his own time.

tlg0526.tlg003.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A polemical defense of Judaism against Hellenistic detractors, focusing on the antiquity of the Jewish people.
tlg0526.tlg003.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work argues for the extreme antiquity of Jewish law and history, citing events from the Exodus onwards.

tlg0526.tlg002.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An autobiography focusing on Josephus's command in Galilee and his subsequent life in Rome.
tlg0526.tlg002.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The narrative begins with Josephus's birth in 37 CE and covers his own lived experiences.

tlg0526.tlg004.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents the Jewish revolt against Rome, in which Josephus was a participant and eyewitness.
tlg0526.tlg004.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The main narrative of the conflict begins with the outbreak of the revolt in 66 CE.

tlg0543.tlg001.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Covers the rise of Rome to Mediterranean hegemony and its conflicts with Carthage and the Hellenistic East.
tlg0543.tlg001.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Begins with the First Punic War (-264) and continues through Polybius's own lifetime and diplomatic career.

tlg0551.tlg009.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Focuses on the three Punic Wars between the Roman Republic and the Carthaginian Empire.
tlg0551.tlg009.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Appian (writing in the 2nd century CE) reconstructs the wars starting from the mid-3rd century BCE.

tlg0551.tlg007.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Narrates the Roman conquest of the Iberian Peninsula and the resistance of local tribes.
tlg0551.tlg007.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The narrative begins with Roman intervention in Spain during the Second Punic War.

tlg0551.tlg001.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The preface outlines the scope of the Roman Empire and Appian's historiographical method.
tlg0551.tlg001.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The preface reflects Appian's own perspective and the extent of the Empire during his lifetime.

tlg0551.tlg006.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Covers the Roman acquisition of Sicily, Sardinia, and Corsica.
tlg0551.tlg006.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Focuses on the First Punic War era when Rome first expanded into the Mediterranean islands.

tlg0551.tlg008.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Specifically details Hannibal's invasion of Italy during the Second Punic War.
tlg0551.tlg008.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Narrates the events of the Second Punic War starting with Hannibal's crossing of the Alps.

tlg0551.tlg012.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Documents the Roman wars against the Illyrians and the eventual provincial organization of the region.
tlg0551.tlg012.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Begins with the First Illyrian War in -229, centuries before Appian's time.

tlg0551.tlg013.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Covers the Roman-Seleucid War and Roman interventions in the Hellenistic East.
tlg0551.tlg013.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Narrates the conflict between Rome and Antiochus III starting in -192.

tlg0007.tlg140.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This polemical work critiques Colotes, a 3rd-century BCE disciple of Epicurus, focusing on the philosophical landscape of the early Hellenistic period.
tlg0007.tlg140.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch critiques a philosopher active in the early 3rd century BCE, roughly 400 years before his own time.

tlg0007.tlg032.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of the Spartan general Lysander during the Peloponnesian War and its aftermath.
tlg0007.tlg032.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Lysander, beginning with his background prior to his command in the late 5th century BCE.

tlg0007.tlg035.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of the Athenian statesman and general Cimon during the Greco-Persian Wars.
tlg0007.tlg035.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Cimon, starting with his family background and birth in the late 6th century BCE.

tlg0007.tlg003.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This comparison evaluates the legendary founders of Athens and Rome, Theseus and Romulus.
tlg0007.tlg003.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work compares figures from the legendary/heroic age, using the traditional Trojan War era as the earliest anchor.

tlg0007.tlg004.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life and legal reforms of the legendary Spartan lawgiver Lycurgus.
tlg0007.tlg004.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the traditional date of the Lycurgan reforms in the 9th or 8th century BCE.

tlg0007.tlg050.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of Cato the Younger, a Stoic politician who opposed Julius Caesar.
tlg0007.tlg050.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs Cato's life from his birth in -95 through his suicide in -46.

tlg0007.tlg057.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of Demetrius Poliorcetes, a key figure among the Successors of Alexander the Great.
tlg0007.tlg057.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Demetrius from his birth in the mid-4th century BCE.

tlg0007.tlg061.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of Marcus Junius Brutus, the leading assassin of Julius Caesar.
tlg0007.tlg061.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the life of Brutus from his birth in -85 through the Battle of Philippi.

tlg0007.tlg066.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_in_work: Roman Empire (Year of the Four Emperors)
tlg0007.tlg066.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the brief reign of the Emperor Otho in 69 CE.
tlg0007.tlg066.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch lived through the events of 69 CE; the narrative focuses on Otho's short-lived imperial tenure.

tlg0007.tlg059.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_in_work: Hellenistic Kingdoms and Late Roman Republic
tlg0007.tlg059.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This comparison evaluates the Macedonian king Demetrius Poliorcetes and the Roman triumvir Mark Antony.
tlg0007.tlg059.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The comparison spans from the birth of Demetrius in the 4th century BCE to the death of Antony in 30 BCE.

tlg0007.tlg005.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life and religious reforms of Numa Pompilius, the second king of Rome.
tlg0007.tlg005.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs the reign of Numa following the traditional founding of Rome.

tlg0007.tlg002.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of Romulus, the legendary founder and first king of Rome.
tlg0007.tlg002.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch uses the traditional date for the founding of Rome as the primary anchor for Romulus's life.

tlg0007.tlg034.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_in_work: Classical Sparta and Late Roman Republic
tlg0007.tlg034.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This comparison evaluates the Spartan general Lysander and the Roman dictator Lucius Cornelius Sulla.
tlg0007.tlg034.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The comparison covers figures from the 5th century BCE (Lysander) and the 1st century BCE (Sulla).

tlg0007.tlg033.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: The work documents the life of the Roman dictator Sulla, focusing on his rise to power and the civil wars.
tlg0007.tlg033.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch reconstructs Sulla's life from his birth in -138 through his death in -78.

tlg0007.tlg067.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This pedagogical treatise reflects the educational values and social norms of the elite in Plutarch's own era.
tlg0007.tlg067.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work is a contemporary philosophical essay on education reflecting the author's own social context.

tlg0007.tlg098.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This Moralia essay addresses universal human ethics within the context of Plutarch's contemporary Graeco-Roman society.
tlg0007.tlg098.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch writes on ethical behavior relevant to his own era; anchor is his floruit around 75 CE.

tlg0007.tlg107.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A philosophical dialogue on divine providence and justice, reflecting the intellectual climate of the Roman Empire.
tlg0007.tlg107.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work is a philosophical reflection on contemporary theological and ethical concerns during Plutarch's lifetime.

tlg0007.tlg102.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An ethical treatise on social conduct and curiosity within the urban society of the Roman Empire.
tlg0007.tlg102.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch provides advice for his contemporaries on avoiding the vice of meddling in others' affairs.

tlg0007.tlg073.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A moral essay discussing the nature of friendship and social circles in the author's contemporary society.
tlg0007.tlg073.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The essay addresses the social habits and ethics of the 1st-century CE Graeco-Roman elite.

tlg0007.tlg072.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Practical ethical advice on personal conduct and rivalry within the Roman imperial social context.
tlg0007.tlg072.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch writes for his contemporaries on how to turn personal enmities into opportunities for self-improvement.

tlg0007.tlg101.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A treatise on the vice of loquacity, using examples from both history and contemporary social life.
tlg0007.tlg101.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work is an ethical guide for Plutarch's own generation regarding social discourse.

tlg0007.tlg120.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An essay warning against the dangers of debt and usury in the economic context of the Roman Empire.
tlg0007.tlg120.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch addresses the financial pressures and social consequences of borrowing in his own time.

tlg0007.tlg077.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A health manual providing dietary and lifestyle advice for the educated class of the Roman Empire.
tlg0007.tlg077.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work reflects the medical knowledge and daily habits of the 1st-century CE Graeco-Roman world.

tlg0016.tlg001.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Herodotus documents the rise of Persia and the conflict between the Greek city-states and the Achaemenid Empire.
tlg0016.tlg001.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The narrative begins with the rise of Croesus of Lydia around -560, predating Herodotus's own era.

tlg0032.tlg009.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A panegyric of the Spartan king Agesilaus II, documenting his military campaigns and character.
tlg0032.tlg009.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Xenophon was a close friend and subordinate of Agesilaus; the work covers events from the king's accession around -400.

tlg0032.tlg007.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A fictionalized biography of Cyrus the Great, the founder of the Persian Empire.
tlg0032.tlg007.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Xenophon reconstructs the life of Cyrus the Great, who reigned in the 6th century BCE, long before Xenophon's time.

tlg0032.tlg006.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An eyewitness account of the expedition of Cyrus the Younger and the retreat of the Ten Thousand Greek mercenaries.
tlg0032.tlg006.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Xenophon participated in the expedition; the narrative begins with the march of Cyrus in -401.

tlg0032.tlg008.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A dialogue between the poet Simonides and the tyrant Hiero I of Syracuse concerning the nature of tyranny.
tlg0032.tlg008.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The dialogue is set during the reign of Hiero I (r. 478–467 BCE), which predates Xenophon's birth.

tlg0032.tlg002.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A collection of Socratic dialogues defending Socrates and recording his conversations in Athens.
tlg0032.tlg002.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Xenophon was a student of Socrates; the work documents Socrates' life and teachings during Xenophon's youth and adulthood.

tlg0032.tlg013.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A technical manual on horse care and riding for the Greek cavalry class.
tlg0032.tlg013.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Xenophon writes from his own experience as a cavalry officer during his floruit in the early 4th century BCE.

tlg0007.tlg103.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: This Moralia essay addresses ethical behavior within the contemporary social context of the Roman Empire.
tlg0007.tlg103.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work is a philosophical discourse reflecting the social values and economic realities of Plutarch's own era (c. 75 CE).

tlg0007.tlg105.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A philosophical treatise on human emotions relevant to the author's contemporary Roman-era audience.
tlg0007.tlg105.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The essay treats universal psychological states from the perspective of a 1st-century CE Middle Platonist.

tlg0007.tlg114.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A collection of romantic anecdotes drawn from various historical and legendary Greek and Roman settings.
tlg0007.tlg114.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The stories are historical or legendary exempla dating back to the Archaic and Classical periods, starting roughly around -600.

tlg0007.tlg075.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A moral essay comparing ethical states within the framework of contemporary philosophical education.
tlg0007.tlg075.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work functions as a contemporary lecture or diatribe on ethics during Plutarch's floruit.

tlg0007.tlg081.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A collection of apophthegms from famous leaders across Greek and Roman history.
tlg0007.tlg081.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The collection begins with figures like Cyrus and Croesus (-560) and spans through the Hellenistic and Republican eras.

tlg0007.tlg100.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A philosophical comparison of mental and physical ailments within the context of contemporary medical and ethical thought.
tlg0007.tlg100.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The discourse addresses the author's contemporaries using the medical and philosophical paradigms of the 1st century CE.

tlg0007.tlg137.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A polemical work critiquing Stoic philosophy, a dominant school in the contemporary Roman Empire.
tlg0007.tlg137.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch engages in contemporary philosophical debate against the Stoics of his own time.

tlg0007.tlg126.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A scientific and philosophical dialogue set among Plutarch's contemporaries discussing astronomy and myth.
tlg0007.tlg126.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The dialogue features the author's friends and family, reflecting the intellectual life of the late 1st century CE.

tlg0007.tlg097.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An ethical treatise on family relations addressed to his contemporaries.
tlg0007.tlg097.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work provides moral guidance for the social and familial structures of the Roman-era Greek world.

tlg0007.tlg093.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A pedagogical essay on the nature of virtue and education in the contemporary world.
tlg0007.tlg093.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The essay addresses the educational concerns of the author's own time.

tlg0007.tlg088.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A rhetorical comparison of the military and intellectual achievements of 5th and 4th-century BCE Athens.
tlg0007.tlg088.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work focuses on the era of the Persian Wars (-490) and the subsequent Golden Age of Athens.

tlg0007.tlg130.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A dialogue between Odysseus and one of Circe's pigs (Gryllus) regarding animal rationality.
tlg0007.tlg130.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The setting is the legendary aftermath of the Trojan War, anchored to the traditional era of Odysseus.

tlg0007.tlg119.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A political treatise comparing constitutional forms using historical examples from Greece and Rome.
tlg0007.tlg119.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work analyzes historical constitutions (past) to provide advice for contemporary political life under Rome.

tlg0007.tlg069.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A guide for young men on how to benefit from philosophical lectures in the contemporary educational system.
tlg0007.tlg069.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work is a practical educational manual for students in Plutarch's own time.

tlg0007.tlg117.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An essay advising elderly elites on their continued role in local and imperial public life.
tlg0007.tlg117.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work addresses the specific civic duties of the Greek aristocracy under the Roman Empire in the 1st century CE.

tlg0007.tlg083.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A collection of historical anecdotes about courageous women from various Greek cities and barbarian nations.
tlg0007.tlg083.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The anecdotes span from the archaic period (e.g., the Trojan women or the Argive women under Telesilla) through the Hellenistic era.

tlg0007.tlg070.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An ethical essay addressed to a Roman contemporary (Philopappus) regarding social conduct in the imperial elite.
tlg0007.tlg070.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work is a philosophical dialogue/essay reflecting the social realities of Plutarch's own lifetime in the 1st century CE.

tlg0007.tlg082.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A collection of apophthegms attributed to famous Spartan kings and citizens from the archaic and classical periods.
tlg0007.tlg082.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The sayings begin with Lycurgus (traditionally dated to the 9th/8th century BCE) and continue through the Spartan decline.

tlg0007.tlg141.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A polemic against the Epicurean maxim 'lathe biōsas', referencing the philosophical schools of the Hellenistic period.
tlg0007.tlg141.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work critiques Epicurus (who founded his school in Athens around -306) and his historical followers.

tlg0007.tlg118.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Advice for young Greeks entering local politics under the overarching administration of the Roman Empire.
tlg0007.tlg118.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: Plutarch addresses the contemporary political situation of Greek notables serving under Roman provincial rule.

tlg0007.tlg110.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A consolatory essay for a friend in exile, drawing on examples from across Greek and Roman history.
tlg0007.tlg110.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: While addressed to a contemporary, it relies heavily on historical examples of exile from the Classical period onwards.

tlg0007.tlg099.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A philosophical treatise on ethics and the nature of happiness within the context of Middle Platonism.
tlg0007.tlg099.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: This is a general ethical essay reflecting the philosophical discourse of Plutarch's own era.

tlg0007.tlg078.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: Practical and moral advice written for a specific contemporary couple (Pollianus and Eurydice).
tlg0007.tlg078.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work is a wedding gift for Plutarch's contemporaries, reflecting 1st-century CE social norms.

tlg0007.tlg112.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A series of table-talk discussions between Plutarch and his friends across various locations in the Empire.
tlg0007.tlg112.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The dialogues record conversations among Plutarch's own circle of friends and family during his lifetime.

tlg0007.tlg068.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An educational treatise on how to use the works of classical poets (Homer, Hesiod, etc.) in a moral education.
tlg0007.tlg068.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work focuses on the interpretation of poets from the 8th century BCE through the 5th century BCE.

tlg0007.tlg116.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A philosophical exhortation addressed to a contemporary political figure or ruler on the necessity of education.
tlg0007.tlg116.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: The essay is directed at the ruling class of the Roman imperial administration during Plutarch's time.

tlg0007.tlg128.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A rhetorical exercise (declamatio) comparing the utility of natural elements.
tlg0007.tlg128.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: This is a timeless rhetorical exercise typical of the Second Sophistic education in Plutarch's day.

tlg0007.tlg104.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An ethical essay on overcoming excessive shyness or 'dysopia' in social and political life.
tlg0007.tlg104.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The work addresses the social psychology and etiquette of the elite in the 1st-century CE Roman world.

tlg0007.tlg082b.perseus-eng2.mentioned_polity_reasoning: A collection of sayings attributed to Spartan women, emphasizing their unique social status and laconic wit.
tlg0007.tlg082b.perseus-eng2.mentioned_time_reasoning: The sayings are attributed to figures from the Persian Wars through the Hellenistic period.

tlg0007.tlg115.perseus-eng3.mentioned_polity_reasoning: An argument that philosophers should influence political leaders to achieve the greatest public good.
tlg0007.tlg115.perseus-eng3.mentioned_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary role of the philosopher as an advisor to Roman provincial and imperial authorities.
